metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC1: Resolve the 'rename test OR documented invariant' fork by mandating a concrete test (e.g., assert the set of registered IDs equals a hardcoded frozenset), removing the unexecutable 'documented invariant' escape hatch.
findings.finding: AC1 offers 'a rename test OR documented invariant' to assert stable IDs, deferring the mechanism choice to the executor. 'Documented invariant' (a code comment or doc file) is not an in-session-executable proving command, so an agent could ship a comment and call AC1 satisfied without any test coverage of ID stability.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a rename test or documented invariant' with: 'a pytest assertion that the set of registered unit IDs equals a hard-coded frozenset, causing CI to fail on any rename.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] E6: Add a concrete proving command to each AC and add one integration AC that runs a full init (or re-init) against a real temp repo and asserts: all 5 IDs appear in .ensure-applied, git log shows no extra commits on second run_ensures(), and the old helper names have zero remaining call sites (grep -rn '_ensure_env_id\|_migrate_gc' src/).
findings.finding: No AC carries a concrete proving command. AC3 mentions 'git-log assertion' without specifying the command; ACs 2, 4, 5, and 6 have no proving commands at all. The new init-wiring cutover (replacing hand-listed calls with run_ensures() at all entry points) has no end-to-end AC that exercises the new path as it will run — e.g., performing an actual init on a fresh or existing repo and asserting .ensure-applied content and git-log output.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2, AC3, AC4, AC5, AC6; What section (cutover wiring)
findings.suggested_fix:
Example commands to embed in ACs:
- AC3: `git -C <tracker> log --oneline | wc -l` before and after second `run_ensures()` — assert equal.
- AC4: `grep -rn '_ensure_env_id\|_ensure_gitattributes\|_ensure_gitignore\|_migrate_gc\|_ensure_merge_driver' src/ | grep -v ensures.py` — assert empty.
- AC5: `python -c "import json; json.load(open('.ensure-applied'))"` — assert list of 5 IDs.
- Integration AC: `pytest tests/test_ensures_integration.py -k 'test_fresh_init_and_reinit'` exercising real git repo init.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Describe in the What section HOW id stability is enforced (e.g., a test that asserts the exact set of registered ids, or a code-level constant list), so the mechanism matches AC1's 'rename test or documented invariant' requirement.
findings.finding: AC1 requires that unit id stability is 'asserted' via 'a rename test or documented invariant', but the What section only declares ids as 'stable, immutable' without describing any enforcement mechanism, test, or invariant. This leaves AC1's verification step without a corresponding described deliverable.
findings.location: AC1 vs What section ('stable, immutable id')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to What: 'A module-level tuple `_STABLE_IDS` lists all registered ids; a unit test asserts `{u.id for u in REGISTRY} == set(_STABLE_IDS)` so any rename fails the test suite immediately.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an end-to-end AC that runs a full fresh-init (and separately a re-init) through `init_core` against a real temp git repo and asserts that all 5 ensure units executed (e.g., by checking `.ensure-applied` contains all 5 ids after init completes), so the wiring — not just `run_ensures()` in isolation — is behaviorally validated.
findings.finding: The plan migrates all init entry points (re-init arm, fresh-init arm, _init_via_symlink) to call `run_ensures()`, making it the defaulted path for all inits, but no AC requires an end-to-end behavioral test that exercises the full init → `run_ensures()` wiring. AC4 only checks structural removal of old call sites ('no other callers remain'), which can be satisfied by a source-grep without ever running init through the new wiring. A broken wiring (e.g., `run_ensures()` not called at the right point, called outside the write-lock, or called with a wrong tracker argument) would leave all ACs green while the live default path is broken.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 / 'Wire run_ensures() at ALL init entry points' in What section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'Verified by an integration test that calls init_core end-to-end on (a) a fresh repo and (b) an existing repo, then reads .ensure-applied and asserts all 5 unit ids are present and no failed outcomes were logged.' This directly exercises the cutover path through the init stack.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile AC4's 'three call sites' count with the What section's 'all entry points' wiring: either explicitly state env-id is intentionally added to fresh-init (making it four paths) or clarify that fresh-init wiring runs all 5 units including env-id and update AC4 to reflect the correct count.
findings.finding: AC4 says '_ensure_env_id's three call sites are all routed through / preserved by the migration', implying exactly three call sites. But the What section says wire run_ensures() at ALL init entry points including the fresh-init arm (242-248), which currently does NOT call env-id. Running run_ensures() at fresh-init would give env-id a fourth execution path not counted in AC4's 'three call sites', creating a contradiction between sections.
findings.location: What section (fresh-init arm wiring) vs AC4 ('_ensure_env_id's three call sites')
findings.suggested_fix: Update AC4 to read: '_ensure_env_id previously had three call sites (re-init, symlink-init ×2); after migration all three are removed and env-id now runs as part of run_ensures() at ALL entry points including fresh-init (safe because env-id is idempotent); confirm no standalone callers remain.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale in the 'Why' or 'What' section explaining (a) why the store write lock (not the init mkdir lock or lockless) is the correct boundary for run_ensures() — e.g., it serializes concurrent re-init callers that have no other mutual exclusion — and (b) why a full-rewrite local JSON array is chosen for .ensure-applied — e.g., it is idempotent, human-readable, and survives rollback without coupling to the tickets branch.
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for the chosen mechanism: why the store write lock (rather than the existing init mkdir lock or lockless execution) is the right synchronization boundary for run_ensures(), and why a full-rewrite JSON array in .ensure-applied fits better than alternatives. The 'Why' section motivates the problem only, not the solution design.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What' section: 'run_ensures() runs under the store write lock because the re-init arm holds no other lock, and the write lock is the only per-store mutual-exclusion primitive that is already present on every host; this serializes concurrent re-inits without introducing a new lock. .ensure-applied is a local full-rewrite JSON array (not a committed file on the tickets branch) because: (1) it is gitignored, surviving rollback cleanly; (2) a full rewrite is idempotent under the write lock; (3) it requires no branch commit or push.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit ordering constraint in the What section (or AC) that run_ensures() is called AFTER all bootstrap steps in the fresh-init arm, so commit-units have their preconditions satisfied.
findings.finding: The plan replaces the fresh-init arm's calls to 4 of the 5 units (gitignore/gitattributes/gc/merge, lines 242-248) with run_ensures(), but does not specify that run_ensures() must be called AFTER the bootstrap steps (_commit_precommit/_gen_local_files/_exclude_scratch/_ensure_branch_user_config) in the fresh-init arm — if called before bootstrap, commit-units that assume bootstrap preconditions (e.g. gitattributes expects certain files to exist) will silently no-op or fail.
findings.location: What section: 'the fresh-init arm (242-248 — gitignore/gitattributes/gc/merge)'; AC line 3 and 6
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'In the fresh-init arm, run_ensures() is called after _commit_precommit, _gen_local_files, _exclude_scratch_in_tracker, and _ensure_branch_user_config have completed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that the `env-id` ensure unit is a no-op on fresh init (because `_gen_local_files` already writes `.env-id` at line 246), and confirm that `_gen_local_files` is NOT modified and that the unit's check-then-act guard (`not os.path.isfile`) handles both paths correctly.
findings.finding: The plan says 'remove the hand-listed calls from the fresh-init arm (242-248 — gitignore/gitattributes/gc/merge)' but the actual fresh-init arm at lines 241-248 contains SIX steps: `_ensure_branch_user_config` (241), `_commit_gitignore` (242), `_exclude_scratch_in_tracker` (243), `_commit_precommit` (244), `_commit_gitattributes` (245), `_gen_local_files` (246), `_migrate_gc_config` (247), `_ensure_merge_ours_driver` (248). The plan's parenthetical 'gitignore/gitattributes/gc/merge' (4 items) matches 4 of the 5 registry units, but omits `env-id` from that enumeration even though `_gen_local_files` (which creates `.env-id` on fresh init) is a bootstrap step that stays. The plan is ambiguous about whether `env-id` ensure unit covers only re-init or also the fresh-init path — `_gen_local_files` does env-id creation on fresh init, while `_ensure_env_id` does it on re-init. If `_ensure_env_id` is migrated into the registry and `_gen_local_files` is kept, the env-id ensure unit must be a no-op on fresh init (file already written by `_gen_local_files`). This overlap is unacknowledged.
findings.location: What section (fresh-init arm 242-248); Scope section; AC line 4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_store/sync.py:17` to the edit-target list and update its cross-reference from `init._migrate_gc_config` to the new `ensures.py` location (or remove the reference) when the helper is migrated.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_store/sync.py` line 17 contains a docstring cross-reference to `init._migrate_gc_config` by name. When the plan removes `_migrate_gc_config` from `init.py` (or renames/privatizes it into `ensures.py`), this cross-reference becomes a stale dead link. The plan does not enumerate `sync.py` as an edit target.
findings.suggested_fix: In sync.py line 17, replace `(see ``init._migrate_gc_config``)` with `(see ``ensures.gc_config`` ensure unit)` or similar, pointing to the new location in `ensures.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the rollback guarantee to explicitly assert the tracker-branch schema state after code rollback: state that the committed changes from the COMMIT-type ensure units (gitignore/.gitattributes additions) are additive and backward-compatible, so old `_commit_gitignore`/`_commit_gitattributes` remain idempotent against the post-migration tracker branch.
findings.finding: The plan's rollback guarantee addresses only the git-ignored `.ensure-applied` marker file, not the tracker branch schema state after a code rollback. The 5 ensure units that commit to the tracker branch (gitattributes, gitignore) leave new commits in the tracker branch history that persist after reverting the new code. The plan does not assert that this post-rollback tracker-branch state is safe for the old code to operate on.
findings.location: What section, final paragraph: 'Post-rollback: old code ignores the git-ignored `.ensure-applied` file (no coupling).'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Post-rollback' sentence: 'The tracker-branch commits made by COMMIT-type units (gitattributes, gitignore) are additive and backward-compatible; the old `_commit_gitignore` / `_commit_gitattributes` check-then-commit logic remains idempotent against the updated tree, so no tracker-branch state cleanup is required on rollback.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit lock-ordering analysis (confirm no caller of init_core holds a conflicting lock before run_ensures() acquires the write lock) and a back-out plan (e.g., if lock acquisition fails, run_ensures() degrades gracefully or the lock can be dropped in a rollback) to the What section.
findings.finding: The What section introduces a new write-lock acquisition inside init_core ('init_core does NOT currently hold it; this is a new dependency') but provides no analysis of whether init_core or its callers already hold any lock that could conflict, and no rollback or back-out plan if the new lock acquisition causes init failures. T4 requires destructive/behavioral changes to carry an explicit rollback mechanism, not just acknowledgement.
findings.location: What section: 'run_ensures(tracker) runs ALL units unconditionally under the STORE write lock… init_core does NOT currently hold it; this is a new dependency'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `write_lock` in `lock.py` is inter-process safe (e.g., flock-based); if it is only a threading lock, add an inter-process guard (or document the assumption that concurrent multi-process init against the same store is not supported).
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether the store write_lock (lock.py) is inter-process safe (e.g., flock-based) or only intra-process (threading.Lock). If two separate init processes run concurrently against the same store, they may each believe they hold the lock, causing a TOCTOU race on .ensure-applied and on the check-then-act units (e.g., duplicate git commits or a torn .ensure-applied file).
findings.location: What section: 'run_ensures(tracker) runs ALL units unconditionally under the STORE write lock (_store/lock.py write_lock)'; AC line 2
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's What section, add: 'The store write_lock MUST be an OS-level exclusive lock (e.g., fcntl.flock) so that concurrent init processes on the same store are serialized. If lock.py already provides this, cite it explicitly; if not, upgrade it as part of WS1.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a timeout to git operations performed inside `run_ensures()` (or to the write_lock acquisition itself) so that a stalled git commit cannot hold the store write lock indefinitely; document the chosen timeout value and the failure behaviour (unit → `failed`, lock released).
findings.finding: run_ensures() holds the store write lock for the entire duration of all 5 units, two of which perform git commits. Git operations can block indefinitely (slow hooks, lock contention inside git, network-mounted repos). No timeout or deadlock-prevention mechanism is specified, meaning an unresponsive git commit could hold the write lock forever and starve all other store operations.
findings.location: What section: 'run_ensures(tracker) runs ALL units unconditionally under the store write lock'; AC line 2 ('a raising unit is caught … never aborts')
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap each unit's callable invocation with a subprocess timeout (e.g., git commands use `timeout=` in subprocess.run) and document that if a unit exceeds N seconds it is treated as `failed` and the lock is released before moving to the next unit — or restructure so git commits happen after the lock is released for non-check operations.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Audit whether any of the 5 migrated helpers currently acquire the store write lock internally and document (or enforce) that run_ensures() callers hold the outer lock while helpers do not re-acquire it.
findings.finding: The plan introduces run_ensures() under the store write lock but does not address whether any of the 5 migrated helpers currently acquire the write lock themselves — if so, wrapping them inside run_ensures() (which holds the write lock) would cause a deadlock or re-entrancy error, a behavioral change not acknowledged for those callers.
findings.location: What section: 'run_ensures(tracker) runs ALL units unconditionally under the STORE write lock'; AC line 2
findings.suggested_fix: In the What/AC section, add an explicit statement: 'Each migrated unit callable must NOT acquire the store write lock (run_ensures holds it); verify none of the 5 current helpers do so before migration.' If any do, strip the inner lock acquisition before migrating.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add one sentence in the Why or What section explicitly citing rebar's stdlib-only core constraint (docs/config.md) as the reason Python-ecosystem desired-state libraries were not reused, and note briefly why the bespoke registry is preferred over keeping the existing linear `_ensure_*`/`_migrate_*` call sequence.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a bespoke desired-state ensure-registry (novel pattern) without documenting that Python-ecosystem libraries implementing the same pattern were considered and rejected. The natural justification — rebar's documented stdlib-only core constraint (docs/config.md lines 48-50: 'engine core and reconciler are otherwise stdlib-only') — exists in the codebase but is absent from the plan text. Additionally, the plan does not argue why the bespoke registry is preferable to keeping the existing linear check-then-act call sequence that already works, beyond the vague 'generalizes them'.
findings.location: Why section ('School B desired-state / convergent'); What section ('Create src/rebar/_store/ensures.py: a registry of ensure units')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Why section, after 'School B desired-state', add: 'A bespoke stdlib-only implementation is required because rebar's engine core is stdlib-only (docs/config.md) — Python-ecosystem convergence libraries (yoyo-migrations, etc.) are not available in the core. The registry generalizes the existing linear _ensure_/_migrate_ call sequence to support future fsck/server wiring (WS3) without duplicating the call list at each entry point.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
